corpus.name: Follow Me — Se Jun Park's 2014 World Champion team
corpus.format: VGC 2014 (Gen 6 XY doubles; Kalos regional dex; bring 6 / pick 4, flat level 50)
corpus.archetype: Redirection & trapping support offense (bulky offense)
corpus.event: 2014 Pokemon World Championships, Masters Division — 1st place (Washington, D.C.)
corpus.author: Se Jun Park (Sejun), South Korea
corpus.strategy: Weaken targets with Pachirisu (Super Fang chip + Nuzzle paralysis) while Follow Me redirects attacks, then clean up with priority Brave Bird Talonflame and a Dragon Dance Mega Gyarados sweep; Gothitelle's Shadow Tag traps and removes specific answers. Typical 4-mon core: Pachirisu + Mega Gyarados + Talonflame + Garchomp, with Gothitelle/Gardevoir flexed to the matchup. VERIFIED: the primary Nugget Bridge report matches every ability, item and moveset exactly (and lists natures/EVs); Park's 1st-place finish and roster are corroborated by the Worlds teams article and pokemon.com.

members.role: Dragon Dance setup sweeper / win condition
Redirection + paralysis support (the iconic pick)
Trapper / disruptor
Fast special attacker / speed control
Physical attacker / Kangaskhan check
Priority sweeper / Quick Guard support

synergies: Pachirisu Follow Me redirects hits away from setup Gyarados; Volt Absorb makes Pachirisu immune to Electric, indirectly covering Gyarados's 4x Electric weakness
Nuzzle paralysis gives the slower sweepers effective speed control
Talonflame Quick Guard blocks priority (Fake Out, Sucker Punch, opposing Gale Wings) on Gyarados setup turns; Super Fang chip lets Brave Bird pick up KOs
Gothitelle Shadow Tag traps and removes dedicated Gyarados answers
Telepathy Gardevoir is immune to its own partner's spread Earthquake
antiSynergies: Mega Gyarados is 4x weak to Electric — the whole plan leans on Pachirisu; remove Pachirisu and the sweepers are exposed
Only one Mega, so the offense hinges on a fragile redirection/trap engine
Swagger is luck-dependent (self-hit confusion risk)

corpus.name: Charizard the Benchwarmer — starmetroid's sun team
corpus.format: VGC 2014 (Gen 6 XY doubles; Kalos regional dex; bring 6 / pick 4, flat level 50)
corpus.archetype: Weather (sun) / bulky good-stuff offense built around Mega Venusaur
corpus.event: Washington State Regional Championships, April 20 2014 — Top 4 (Masters)
corpus.author: starmetroid
corpus.strategy: Charizard-Y forces opponents to prepare for sun, but Mega Venusaur (Thick Fat, bulky, Sleep Powder) is the true win condition — the author reads the matchup and picks flexible leads rather than a fixed combo, treating Charizard largely as a bluff. VERIFIED: primary Nugget Bridge report confirms author, Top 4 placement, date, and all six sets. NOTE: the report itself literally lists Salamence's ability as 'Levitate', which is illegal (Salamence can only have Intimidate or Moxie) — corrected to Intimidate below.

members.role: Primary Mega sweeper / disruptor
Bulky attacker / burn support; checks opposing Mega Charizard-Y and Steels
Bulky pivot / mixed attacker

members.ability: Intimidate (report text lists 'Levitate', which is illegal for Salamence and corrected here to Intimidate)
members.role: Fast special attacker / speed control
Sun setter / special attacker / sun bluff
Physical attacker

synergies: Charizard-Y's Drought bluff makes opponents mis-prepare; Mega Venusaur's Thick Fat + bulk wall Fire/Ice and disrupt with Sleep Powder
Rotom-Heat directly checks opposing Mega Charizard-Y and the Steel-types that wall Venusaur; Will-O-Wisp cripples physical threats like Mega Kangaskhan
Aegislash + Rotom-Heat form a defensive backbone while Scarf Salamence and Garchomp provide fast offense, Intimidate and Rough Skin pressure
antiSynergies: Two Mega Stones (Venusaurite + Charizardite Y) but only one Pokemon can Mega Evolve per battle — you cannot have Mega Venusaur AND Charizard-Y's Drought online at once; Charizard is effectively a bluff / used only in games you don't Mega Venusaur
Because the true Mega is usually Venusaur, the team's 'sun' is deliberately inconsistent — Charizard stays base and weaker when Venusaur takes the Mega slot

corpus.name: What's Protect? — Alex Ogloza's US Nationals Champion team
corpus.format: VGC 2014 (Gen 6 XY doubles; Kalos regional dex; bring 6 / pick 4, flat level 50)
corpus.archetype: Rain hyper offense (all-out offense with zero Protect)
corpus.event: 2014 US National Championships, Masters Division — 1st place (def. Adib Alam in finals)
corpus.author: Alex Ogloza
corpus.strategy: Pure hyper offense that famously runs no Protect on any Pokemon: put two fast, rain-boosted attackers on the field turn 1 and out-damage the opponent before they stabilize, using triple Choice items + Assault Vest to force commitment and U-turn/Fake Out for the tempo Protect normally provides. Typical 4-mon core: Politoed + Ludicolo + Mega Kangaskhan + Talonflame or Hydreigon. VERIFIED: 1st-place finish corroborated by Liquipedia, pokemon.com, the finals VOD and the Nugget Bridge Nationals teams recap (which lists the identical six-mon roster); the report explicitly confirms no member runs Protect.

members.role: Rain setter / fast special attacker
Rain sweeper / Fake Out support
Priority attacker / U-turn pivot
Mega sweeper / Fake Out
Bulky special attacker / King's Shield pivot
Special attacker / cleaner

synergies: Drizzle (Politoed) + Swift Swim (Ludicolo) = instant turn-1 speed advantage; Fake Out from Ludicolo/Kangaskhan buys tempo without Protect
Rain removes Aegislash's 2x Fire weakness and softens opposing Fire attacks
Talonflame U-turn pivots and repositions in place of Protect; Gale Wings Brave Bird supplies priority; Mega Kangaskhan Power-Up Punch snowballs
Choice Scarf Politoed is fast enough to survive/answer Talonflame Brave Bird and re-set rain in a weather war
antiSynergies: No Protect on the whole team (Aegislash's King's Shield is the only protective move) — the team cannot scout, stall Trick Room, or dodge double-targeting, so it is very exposed to spread damage
Rain weakens the team's own Fire coverage (Talonflame Flare Blitz)
Heavy Choice-locking risks being walled or forced out; opposing weather (Drought/Sand) flips Ludicolo's Swift Swim advantage

corpus.name: The Power of Pledge — Serapis's swamp-terrain offense
corpus.format: VGC 2014 (Gen 6 XY doubles; Kalos regional dex; bring 6 / pick 4, flat level 50)
corpus.archetype: Terrain-based speed control offense (Pledge 'swamp' — non-weather, non-Trick-Room field control)
corpus.event: Philadelphia Regional Championships 2014 — made top cut (7-2 Swiss record; eliminated in the first elimination round)
corpus.author: Jeremy Rodrigues (Serapis)
corpus.strategy: Lead Greninja + Mega Venusaur and fire Water Pledge + Grass Pledge together to create a swamp that halves all opposing Speed for 3 turns, then sweep with normally-slower attackers (Dragon Dance Haxorus, Specs Chandelure) that now outspeed. Well-documented with full sets, but a niche top-cut regional team rather than a championship staple. Typical 4-mon core: Greninja + Mega Venusaur + Haxorus + Chandelure. VERIFIED against the single primary Nugget Bridge report, which matches all sets and confirms the documented Zapdos item mistake; single-source and niche, hence medium confidence. Softened the original 'Top 32' claim to 'made top cut' as the exact cut size was not stated in the source.

members.role: Fast Pledge partner / disruptor
Mega / Pledge partner / sweeper-disruptor
Special attacker (thrives under the swamp speed drop; ignores screens/Substitute)
Dragon Dance physical sweeper / win condition under swamp
Alternate Mega / special attacker / Quick Guard support

members.item: Power Belt (author's documented in-event mistake — he intended Leftovers and forgot to swap it)
members.role: Bulky Electric support / speed control

synergies: Water Pledge (Greninja) + Grass Pledge (Venusaur) fired together = ~160 BP Grass hit plus a swamp that lowers all opposing Speed 50% for 3 turns — artificial speed control without weather or Trick Room
Under the swamp, Life Orb Dragon Dance Haxorus and Choice Specs Chandelure suddenly outspeed and sweep; Infiltrator Chandelure ignores screens and Substitute
Venusaur Sleep Powder neutralizes setup threats like Mega Mawile; Lucario Quick Guard blocks priority (e.g. Sucker Punch) on vulnerable setup turns
antiSynergies: Two Mega Stones (Venusaurite + Lucarionite) but only one Mega per battle — Mega Lucario and Mega Venusaur compete for the single slot
The swamp lasts only 3 turns and requires both Pledge users alive and moving in the correct order — a fragile plan that collapses if one is KO'd or redirected
Zapdos ran the wrong item at the event (Power Belt instead of the intended Leftovers), a mistake the author openly documents

corpus.name: Shoma Honami's World Champion team ("shadeVIERA")
corpus.format: VGC 2015 (Gen 6 ORAS) — Doubles, bring 6 / pick 4
corpus.archetype: Balance / dual-mode with Trick Room support (redirection + weather + speed control)
corpus.author: Shoma Honami (shadeVIERA / SHADEviera), Japan
corpus.event: 1st Place — 2015 Pokémon World Championships (Masters Division, Boston)
corpus.strategy: A Mega Kangaskhan 'good stuff' balance built to flip between a fast mode (Kangaskhan/Landorus/Thundurus) and a Trick Room mode (Cresselia/Amoonguss/Heatran), using Amoonguss redirection and Cresselia's Trick Room to shelter a bulky, hard-hitting core. Amoonguss's Sunny Day both boosts Heatran's Heat Wave and shuts down opposing Politoed rain, which is how the team beat weather squads.

members.role: Primary physical attacker / Fake Out support
Special attacker / defensive pivot (sun-boosted)
Speed control / disruption
Intimidate bulky attacker / special wall
Trick Room setter / bulky support
Redirection / disruption / weather setter

synergies: Amoonguss Rage Powder redirects attacks off Kangaskhan and Heatran, buying setup/Trick Room turns
Cresselia Trick Room flips speed so slow Amoonguss/Heatran/Cresselia move first (dual-mode team)
Amoonguss Sunny Day boosts Heatran's Heat Wave and neutralizes opposing Drizzle/rain teams
Thundurus Safety Goggles makes it immune to Spore/Rage Powder and opposing powder moves
Double bulk backbone (Landorus-T Assault Vest + Cresselia + Amoonguss Regenerator) absorbs hits while Kangaskhan cleans
antiSynergies: Fast mode (Kangaskhan/Landorus/Thundurus) vs Trick Room mode is a tension the pilot must resolve per matchup — you rarely want both up at once
Heatran is 4x weak to Ground (mitigated only by Shuca Berry); stacks a Ground vulnerability the opponent can pressure
Sunny Day is dead weight in games where Heatran isn't brought

corpus.name: Hideyuki Taida's Worlds Runner-Up team ("BIDC")
corpus.format: VGC 2015 (Gen 6 ORAS) — Doubles, bring 6 / pick 4
corpus.archetype: Bulky offense / Trick Room setup (Weakness Policy + Quiver Dance win conditions)
corpus.author: Hideyuki Taida (BIDC), Japan
corpus.event: 2nd Place — 2015 Pokémon World Championships (Masters Division, Boston)
corpus.strategy: A Kangaskhan balance that leans on setup sweepers under Cresselia's Trick Room: Weakness Policy Aegislash bluffs with King's Shield and turns a super-effective hit into a +2/+2 sweep, while Quiver Dance Volcarona snowballs. Cresselia enables the slow attackers and Thundurus/Landorus provide fast-mode speed control.

members.role: Trick Room setter / bulky support
Weakness Policy setup sweeper / bulky attacker
Quiver Dance setup sweeper
Fast offense / Taunt disruption
Physical attacker / Fake Out + Power-Up Punch snowball
Intimidate attacker / Focus Sash lead

synergies: Cresselia Trick Room lets slow Aegislash and post-Quiver Volcarona move first
Aegislash King's Shield stalls while Weakness Policy waits to convert a super-effective hit into a +2/+2 attacker
Kangaskhan Power-Up Punch + Sucker Punch gives a self-boosting physical win condition alongside the special sweepers
Intimidate (Landorus-T) plus Icy Wind (Cresselia) soften physical attackers and add extra speed control
antiSynergies: Weakness Policy Aegislash is conditional — it needs the opponent to land a super-effective hit, which they can decline
Fast pieces (Thundurus, Kangaskhan) fight the Trick Room mode; committing to Trick Room can strand them
Volcarona and Aegislash both crave setup turns but the team can usually protect only one at a time

corpus.name: 20XX Mence (Alberto Lara / CaliSweeper)
corpus.format: VGC 2015 (Gen 6 ORAS) — Doubles, bring 6 / pick 4
corpus.archetype: Dual-mega hyper offense / bulky offense hybrid (Salamence + Charizard-Y)
corpus.author: Alberto Lara (CaliSweeper / Sweeper), USA
corpus.event: 2x Regional Champion (SoCal & Utah) and 24th of 418 (Top 24) — U.S. National Championships 2015
corpus.strategy: A flexible offense carrying two mega options — Dragon Dance Mega Salamence and Drought Mega Charizard-Y — so the pilot picks the mega that best fits the matchup, all propped up by Ferrothorn as the defensive backbone that grinds teams down with Leech Seed and Iron Barbs. Spread-move leads (Charizard-Y Heat Wave + Sylveon Hyper Voice) pressure both targets at once.

members.role: Dragon Dance sweeper / Intimidate
Sun/Drought special attacker (alternate mega)
Pixilate spread attacker / Helping Hand support
Defensive backbone / Leech Seed staller
Choice Scarf speed control / Intimidate pivot
Protean fast coverage attacker

synergies: Charizard-Y + Sylveon lead spams unavoidable spread damage (Heat Wave + Hyper Voice) across both opposing slots
Ferrothorn's Steel/Grass typing walls Fairy, Water and Rock threats that trouble the offense, then chips with Leech Seed + Iron Barbs
Double Intimidate (Salamence + Landorus-T) blunts physical attackers turn after turn
Choice Scarf Landorus-T supplies speed control and pivots with U-turn to bring in the right attacker
antiSynergies: Two megas but only one may be used per battle — Salamence and Charizard-Y are mutually exclusive, so half the mega investment sits on the bench each game
Ferrothorn is 4x weak to Fire and dislikes Charizard-Y's own Drought sun, so the two are rarely on the field together
Greninja and Ferrothorn overlap defensively little; the team can feel thin against fast special attackers if Ferrothorn is removed

corpus.name: When It Rains, It Pours (Jack Hatch / Logilink)
corpus.format: VGC 2015 (Gen 6 ORAS) — Doubles, bring 6 / pick 4
corpus.archetype: Rain hyper offense (weather: rain)
corpus.author: Jack Hatch (Logilink), USA
corpus.event: 1st Place (11-1, undefeated Swiss) — Raleigh Fall Premier Challenge; Top 4 — Cornelius Premier Challenge, 2015 season
corpus.strategy: A rain hyper-offense that opens with Politoed's Drizzle to double Ludicolo's Speed via Swift Swim and power up rain-boosted Scald, then closes games fast with priority and speed control. Terrakion, Scizor and Thundurus provide priority/Quick Guard, Bullet Punch and Thunder Wave, while Mega Salamence is a rain-independent secondary win condition for games where the rain plan is answered.

members.role: Rain setter / Helping Hand support
Swift Swim rain sweeper
Speed control / Taunt disruption
Focus Sash attacker / Quick Guard support
Bullet Punch priority / Swords Dance sweeper
Rain-independent secondary win condition / Intimidate

synergies: Politoed Drizzle doubles Ludicolo's Speed (Swift Swim) and boosts rain Scald while weakening opposing Fire moves
Ludicolo Fake Out + Thundurus Thunder Wave/Taunt lock down opposing speed and setup on the turns rain matters most
Scizor Bullet Punch and Terrakion Quick Guard cover the priority/anti-priority axis rain-offense needs to finish games
Mega Salamence gives a non-weather win condition, so the team isn't dead if rain is removed
antiSynergies: Mega Salamence (Aerilate/physical) gains nothing from rain and its Flamethrower is weakened by it — Salamence mode and rain mode pull opposite directions
Only one weather can be up; opposing Drought/Sand/Drizzle setters can flip the field and neutralize the Swift Swim plan
Ludicolo is fragile off-rain, so removing Politoed collapses the primary sweeper

corpus.name: Wolfe Glick — 2016 World Champion (Kyogre/Rayquaza + Lightning Rod Raichu)
corpus.format: VGC 2016 (Doubles, ORAS; one restricted + Megas/Primals allowed)
corpus.archetype: Restricted bulky offense / rain + redirection with a Trick Room backup mode
corpus.author: Wolfe Glick (USA)
corpus.event: 2016 Pokémon World Championships, Masters Division — 1st place (San Francisco)
corpus.strategy: Bulky Primal Kyogre fires Origin Pulse/Water Spout in perpetual rain while Assault Vest Lightning Rod Raichu absorbs the Electric attacks that are Kyogre's only real answer, redirecting Thunder off it and adding Fake Out/Nuzzle chip. Mega Rayquaza is the setup sweeper (Swords Dance + Extreme Speed priority) and Bronzong provides a Trick Room / Safeguard 'plan B' against Dark Void and speed control. (Team co-built with Markus Stadter, who took the same list to the semifinals.)

members.ability: Delta Stream (base Air Lock as listed on official roster)
members.role: Physical setup sweeper / priority; Jolly, EVs 4 HP / 252 Atk / 252 Spe
Primary special attacker / rain setter; Modest, EVs 204 HP / 204 Def / 44 SpA / 4 SpD / 52 Spe
Trapper / disruptor — Taunt shuts down Geomancy, Trick Room and Tailwind; Timid, EVs 92 HP / 20 Def / 172 SpA / 4 SpD / 220 Spe
Support / Intimidate + Fake Out + Wide Guard pivot; Adamant, EVs 252 HP / 132 Atk / 76 Def / 44 SpD / 4 Spe
Electric-immune redirector protecting Kyogre; paralysis + Endeavor chip; Timid, EVs 36 HP / 204 Def / 20 SpA / 68 SpD / 180 Spe
Trick Room setter / Safeguard vs Dark Void; Sassy, EVs 252 HP / 68 Def / 188 SpD

synergies: Lightning Rod Raichu redirects Electric moves (Kyogre's main weakness) and enables Kyogre's rain-boosted Water Spout to fire safely
Primordial Sea rain nullifies enemy Fire moves and boosts Kyogre's Water attacks
Bronzong Safeguard + Mega Gengar Taunt both neutralize the Smeargle+Xerneas / Dark Void openings that defined the format
Hitmontop Fake Out + Intimidate + Wide Guard buys setup turns for Kyogre and Rayquaza
antiSynergies: Kyogre's Primordial Sea and Mega Rayquaza's Delta Stream are competing weathers — the two restricteds cannot both project their weather at once, so they are largely used in different game plans
Rayquaza's Dragon/Flying coverage gains nothing from rain, so bringing both restricteds together dilutes each other's boosts

corpus.name: Jonathan 'Ezrael' Evans — 2016 Worlds Finalist (Double Primal)
corpus.format: VGC 2016 (Doubles, ORAS; one restricted + Megas/Primals allowed)
corpus.archetype: Double-Primal weather offense / goodstuffs (with Mega Gengar and Mega Manectric options)
corpus.author: Jonathan Evans (USA)
corpus.event: 2016 Pokémon World Championships, Masters Division — 2nd place (San Francisco); lost the final to Wolfe Glick
corpus.strategy: A 'Double Primal' build running both Primal Kyogre and (Red Orb) Primal Groudon so Evans can choose which weather to project each match, backed by two Mega options — trapping Mega Gengar and pivoting Lightning Rod Mega Manectric — plus Bronzong Trick Room and Gale Wings Talonflame for speed control. One of very few top teams to use Mega Manectric.

members.role: Trapper / disruptor; Timid, EVs 76 HP / 240 Def / 4 SpD / 188 Spe
Bulky rain attacker; Modest, EVs 252 HP / 252 Def / 4 SpA
Trick Room / Safeguard support; Relaxed, EVs 252 HP / 188 Def / 68 SpD
Mixed sun attacker; Quiet, EVs 188 HP / 44 Atk / 156 SpA / 68 SpD / 52 Spe
Fast Electric-immune pivot / redirector; Timid, EVs 36 HP / 80 Def / 168 SpA / 220 Spe
Priority attacker / Tailwind speed control; Jolly, EVs 252 HP / 108 Atk / 76 Def / 20 SpD / 52 Spe

synergies: Two Primals let Evans pick the winning weather in the mirror and against rain/sun teams during Team Preview
Lightning Rod Manectric absorbs Electric moves aimed at Primal Kyogre and pivots with Volt Switch to bring in the right attacker
Bronzong Trick Room + Talonflame Tailwind give both slow and fast speed-control modes; Safeguard blocks Dark Void
Mega Gengar Shadow Tag traps and removes fragile support (e.g. opposing redirectors) for the Primals to clean up
antiSynergies: Two Mega Stones (Gengarite + Manectite) but only one Mega-Evolution is allowed per battle — the two megas are mutually exclusive picks
Primal Kyogre's Primordial Sea and Primal Groudon's Desolate Land are opposite weathers that override each other, so the two restricteds are rarely optimal together on the field

corpus.name: Aaron Traylor — 2016 Worlds Top 8 (Dual-setter Trick Room Xerneas/Groudon)
corpus.format: VGC 2016 (Doubles, ORAS; one restricted + Megas/Primals allowed)
corpus.archetype: Trick Room bulky offense (semi-room) with Xerneas + Primal Groudon
corpus.author: Aaron Traylor (USA)
corpus.event: 2016 Pokémon World Championships, Masters Division — 5th place (Top 8); lone undefeated player after Day 2 Swiss
corpus.strategy: One of the only teams to run both Cresselia and Bronzong so a Trick Room setter is available almost every game, letting Traylor field a slower, bulkier Bold Xerneas and Adamant Primal Groudon that outmatch faster builds under Trick Room. Smeargle provides Dark Void plus Wide Guard / Crafty Shield / Spiky Shield protection support, and Mega Salamence offers an Intimidate + Tailwind non-Room mode.

members.role: Primary Trick Room setter / bulky support; Modest
Restricted setup sweeper (slow, Bold — thrives under Trick Room)
Physical wallbreaker / TR attacker; Adamant
Disruption / protection support (this set runs Crafty Shield/Wide Guard, not Follow Me)
Intimidate attacker + non-Room Tailwind mode; Hasty
Secondary Trick Room setter / redundancy; Sassy

synergies: Cresselia + Bronzong = two Trick Room setters, so Trick Room is reliably up despite Taunt/pressure, enabling deliberately slow Xerneas and Groudon
Smeargle's protection moves (Wide Guard, Crafty Shield, Spiky Shield) shield Xerneas while it sets up Geomancy; Dark Void threatens setup turns
Skill Swap (Cresselia) can hand off Levitate or steal a problematic ability; Gravity (Bronzong) boosts Hypnosis/Precipice Blades accuracy
antiSynergies: Mega Salamence's Tailwind (fast mode) directly conflicts with the team's Trick Room (slow mode) — the two speed-control plans cannot be used together and force an all-in commitment each game
A Bold/Adamant slow Xerneas and Groudon are liabilities in any game where Trick Room fails to go up

corpus.name: The 'Big 6' — the format-defining VGC 2016 hyper-offense archetype
corpus.format: VGC 2016 (Doubles, ORAS; one restricted + Megas/Primals allowed)
corpus.archetype: Hyper offense / 'goodstuffs' (Geomancy Xerneas + Primal Groudon)
corpus.author: Community archetype (no single creator; dominated the early 2016 metagame — swept Virginia Regionals Top Cut per VGC With Hats)
corpus.event: Dominant across VGC 2016 Regionals/MSS pre-Winter; the benchmark team the whole format was built to use, beat, or counter
corpus.strategy: Lead Smeargle + Xerneas: Smeargle uses Fake Out and Follow Me to guarantee a free Geomancy, then Xerneas, Primal Groudon and a Mega (Kangaskhan or Salamence) overwhelm with raw power, while Gale Wings Talonflame is the priority Brave Bird 'win button' and Tailwind backup. Called the 'new CHALK' of 2016 — nearly every top team was Big 6 or a Big-6 counter. Exact sets vary between builds since this is a community archetype, not a single documented list.

members.role: Primary attacker; Desolate Land nullifies enemy Water; Adamant, EVs 252 HP / 252 Atk / 4 SpD (set varies by build)
Restricted +2 setup sweeper via one-turn Geomancy; Timid, EVs 4 HP / 252 SpA / 252 Spe
Double-hitting physical attacker / Fake Out pressure; Jolly, EVs 4 HP / 252 Atk / 252 Spe
Special/mixed attacker for the Groudon mirror; Naive, EVs 4 Atk / 252 SpA / 252 Spe
Lead support — Fake Out + Follow Me enable free Geomancy; Timid, EVs 252 HP / 4 Def / 252 Spe
Priority 'win button' + Tailwind speed control; Adamant, EVs 4 HP / 252 Atk / 252 Spe

synergies: Smeargle Fake Out + Follow Me on one Pokémon gives Xerneas a nearly guaranteed Geomancy turn — the core engine of the team
Primal Groudon's Desolate Land removes opposing Water attacks and powers Fire coverage, covering Xerneas's Steel/Poison checks
Gale Wings Talonflame Brave Bird punishes anyone trying to KO a Big 6 member and offers priority + Tailwind as a second speed-control layer
Intimidate (Salamence/Kangaskhan pre-Mega) plus Fake Out blunts opposing physical offense while the sweepers set up
antiSynergies: Carries two Mega Stones (Kangaskhanite + Salamencite) but only one Mega-Evolution is legal per battle — the two megas are mutually exclusive per game
The Smeargle + Xerneas lead is checked by Bronzong + Dual Primals: a Trick Room setter that can punish the slow setup lead, which is how the format learned to beat Big 6 by Spring Regionals

corpus.name: Ryota Otsubo — 2017 World Champion (dual-Z Whimsicott tempo offense)
corpus.format: VGC 2017 (Gen 7 Sun/Moon Doubles, Alola Pokedex only — bring 6 / battle 4)
corpus.archetype: Bulky offense / hyper-tempo (fast Z-move offense, no defensive pinch berries)
corpus.author: Ryota 'barudoru' Otsubo (Japan)
corpus.era: Gen 7 (Sun/Moon), 2017 season. VGC 2017 was Alola-dex-only: NO Mega Evolution and NO restricted/box legendaries. The Tapus and Ultra Beasts (themselves legendary-class) WERE legal and defined the meta; Z-Crystals were legal and central.
corpus.event: 2017 Pokemon World Championships, Masters Division — 1st place (Anaheim Convention Center, Aug 18-20 2017)
corpus.confidence: high — verified against Pokemon.com official recap, Nimbasa City Post Worlds results, and the Limitless VGC team page; all six members and the signature tech (Normalium Z Whimsicott dual-terrain Z-Nature Power, Tapu Fini Ice Beam, Marowak Brick Break, Krookodile Taunt, Celesteela Leech Seed) confirmed by multiple independent sources
corpus.strategy: An offense-first, tempo team that skipped the format's standard defensive 'pinch berries' entirely and loaded damage items (two Z-Crystals, Choice Specs, Life Orb, Alolan Marowak's Thick Club) to knock things out before opponents could heal or set Aurora Veil. Its signature was a dual-terrain Whimsicott whose Prankster-boosted Z-Nature Power turned into a different Z-move depending on which of the two Tapu had terrain up.

members.role: Fast special attacker + Electric Terrain setter (feeds Whimsicott's terrain-based Z-Nature Power)
Bulky Water + Misty Terrain setter; Ice Beam was an off-meta finals tech that netted crucial KOs
Fire/Ghost physical attacker; Brick Break specifically to shatter opposing Aurora Veil / screens
Anti-heal / anti-setup Taunt attacker — a rare pick that weekend, shut down recovery and screens
Support/flex attacker; Prankster gives priority, and Z-Nature Power resolves into a different Z-move under Electric vs Misty terrain
Defensive wall / staller — out-stalled an opposing team in the late rounds

synergies: Two Tapu (Koko + Fini) each set their own terrain so Whimsicott's Prankster Z-Nature Power flips between two different Z-attacks on demand
Krookodile's Taunt + Marowak's Brick Break jointly answer the format's defensive/Aurora-Veil setup cores (the exact style he beat in the final)
All-offense itemization (Z-Crystals/Specs/Life Orb/Thick Club) races opponents before pinch-berry recovery comes online
Celesteela + Tapu Fini provide a slow bulky backbone so the offense can commit hard
antiSynergies: No defensive pinch berries at all — the team is fragile to being out-slowed and ground down (Otsubo himself acknowledged trouble vs Aurora Veil + recovery stalls)
Leans on winning the tempo race; against equally fast Tapu Koko mirrors or Tailwind it can be raced back

corpus.name: Sam Pandelis — 2017 Worlds Runner-up (Aurora Veil defensive setup)
corpus.format: VGC 2017 (Gen 7 Sun/Moon Doubles, Alola Pokedex only — bring 6 / battle 4)
corpus.archetype: Balance / defensive setup ('veil offense' — Aurora Veil + stat-boost win conditions)
corpus.author: Sam 'Zelda' Pandelis (Australia)
corpus.era: Gen 7 (Sun/Moon), 2017 season. Alola-dex-only format (no Megas, no restricted legendaries; Tapus/Ultra Beasts and Z-Crystals legal).
corpus.event: 2017 Pokemon World Championships, Masters Division — 2nd place (Anaheim, Aug 18-20 2017)
corpus.confidence: high — the full six-member sets (items, moves, abilities) match the Nimbasa City Post team analysis exactly, and the top-line strategy is corroborated by the Pokemon.com official recap
corpus.strategy: Trade immediate power for survivability: Alolan Ninetales sets Aurora Veil (extended to 8 turns via Light Clay) while Arcanine's Intimidate and speed control keep the team alive, then setup sweepers (Garchomp Swords Dance, Xurkitree Tail Glow, Tapu Lele Calm Mind) accumulate boosts behind the screen and run away with the game.

members.role: Screen/hail setter + speed control; Light Clay pushes Aurora Veil to 8 turns
Bulky Intimidate support; Bulldoze speed control + Helping Hand to fuel sweepers
Physical setup sweeper (Swords Dance + Z-Earthquake / Tectonic Rage)
Bulky Calm Mind win-con + Psychic Terrain (blocks priority, powers Psyshock)
Defensive pivot / Tailwind + Taunt disruption; Psychic Seed pops in Lele's terrain for +SpDef
Special setup sweeper — Tail Glow behind Aurora Veil is the primary win condition

synergies: Aurora Veil (8 turns via Light Clay) buys the setup turns for Garchomp/Xurkitree/Tapu Lele to boost safely
Tapu Lele's Psychic Terrain triggers Mandibuzz's Psychic Seed for a free +1 SpDef and blocks opposing priority into the setup
Arcanine Intimidate + Bulldoze + Helping Hand is triple-purpose support (bulk, speed control, damage boost) for the sweepers
Mandibuzz Tailwind is a second speed-control layer independent of the screen
antiSynergies: Whole plan depends on Aurora Veil sticking — Brick Break / screen-removal or Taunt on Ninetales collapses the strategy (exactly how Otsubo beat it in the final)
Slow, boost-reliant tempo loses the race to all-out offense that KOs through the screen before boosts matter
Snow Warning hail can be overwritten by opposing weather (Drought/Sand/Drizzle), and terrain is contested by other Tapu

corpus.name: AFK / 'FAKEPG' core (Arcanine-Tapu Fini-Kartana bulky balance)
corpus.format: VGC 2017 (Gen 7 Sun/Moon Doubles, Alola Pokedex only — bring 6 / battle 4)
corpus.archetype: Bulky balance / defensive control (a defining metagame archetype of VGC 2017)
corpus.author: Community metagame archetype (no single author) — 'AFK' core popularized across the season; the exact six here matches the 'Modern-ish FAKEPG' sample list by Rakom in the Smogon VGC 17 sample-teams thread
corpus.era: Gen 7 (Sun/Moon), 2017 season. Alola-dex-only format (no Megas, no restricted legendaries; Tapus/Ultra Beasts and Z-Crystals legal).
corpus.event: Ubiquitous throughout the 2017 season and at Worlds (Tapu Koko ~54% and Arcanine ~38% were among the format's most-used Pokemon); this is the archetype template, not one player's exact list
corpus.confidence: medium — AFK/FAKEPG is a genuinely documented archetype (Nimbasa City Post 'AFK core' feature; Smogon 'Modern-ish FAKEPG' by Rakom uses this exact six; Trainer Tower April-May meta snapshot confirms the Arcanine+Koko usage backbone). Movesets/items are representative sample sets rather than one titlist's list
corpus.strategy: A bulky Fire/Water/Steel-grass control core: Arcanine's Intimidate + Snarl and Tapu Fini's Misty Terrain strip the opponent's physical and special damage and shut off status, which effectively makes Assault Vest Kartana un-KOable while it chips everything; the team slows the game to a crawl and grinds out defensive teams.

members.role: Damage-suppression support (Intimidate + Snarl weaken both attacking categories)
Bulky Calm Mind win-condition + Misty Terrain (blocks status on grounded mons)
Primary attacker; AV + team's damage suppression make it absurdly hard to KO
Offensive pressure / Electric Terrain — the 'K' in FAKEPG; adds the speed and power the slow core lacks
Bulky pivot; enables a Trick Room mode for slower, more defensive variants of the team
Sand setter to override opposing weather (Alolan Ninetales / Pelipper / Torkoal) + bulky Rock attacker

synergies: Arcanine Intimidate + Snarl lowers opposing Atk and SpA so the whole core takes far less damage — the engine that makes AV Kartana tanky
Tapu Fini Misty Terrain shuts off Will-O-Wisp/Toxic/Thunder Wave on grounded mons, protecting the slow bulky core
Gigalith's Sand overwrites opposing weather (a hard answer to Ninetales Aurora Veil and Torkoal sun)
Porygon2 gives an optional Trick Room gear so the same six can play fast-Koko or slow-TR depending on matchup
antiSynergies: Very passive — dedicated setup/stall (e.g. bulky Aurora Veil + Calm Mind Tapu Fini mirrors) can out-grind it
Sand (Gigalith) fights Tapu Fini/Koko's own terrain goals if mismanaged, and Kartana is scared of the format's abundant Fire (Arcanine, Ninetales, Marowak) and Flying coverage
Fits a fast-Koko game and a slow-TR game into one list, so mis-sequencing terrain/Trick Room can brick a turn

NOTE: the broader Torkoal/Lilligant sun archetype also won the 2017 Latin America Internationals in the hands of Ashton Cox — but Cox's exact six (which used Pheromosa, Snorlax, Mimikyu, Tapu Lele) differed from this specific list.
corpus.era: Gen 7 (Sun/Moon), 2017 season. Alola-dex-only format (no Megas, no restricted legendaries; Tapus/Ultra Beasts and Z-Crystals legal).
corpus.event: VGC 2017 season report — documented placements including Hartford Regional (25th, 6-2) for Jean-Marc Hébert and multiple regional finishes for Logan Mazur. corpus.strategy: Torkoal's Drought sets sun, which both super-charges its own Eruption/Heat Wave and doubles Lilligant's Speed via Chlorophyll so she can fire off turn-one Sleep Powder and Quiver Dance; Oricorio-Pom-Pom's Dancer copies every Quiver Dance for a second snowballing sweeper.

members.role: Sun setter + nuke (Quiet, min Speed / max HP-SpA); Solar Beam is instant in sun
Sun sweeper / speed control; doubled Speed enables turn-1 Sleep Powder and After You support
Dancer auto-copies Lilligant's Quiver Dance to become a second boosted sweeper
Psychic Terrain special attacker / Z-Psychic nuke; blocks priority into the sun setup (report lists Skill Swap as a Dazzling Gleam alternative)
Scarf speed control / Rock Slide vs opposing Fire and Flying answers to the sun core
Bulky physical attacker / secondary win-con that appreciates the offensive backbone

synergies: Drought (Torkoal) simultaneously powers Torkoal's Fire moves, makes Solar Beam instant, and doubles Lilligant's Speed via Chlorophyll
Oricorio's Dancer copies Lilligant's Quiver Dance for free, snowballing two sweepers off one setup turn
Lilligant's After You + Sleep Powder is dedicated speed control/disruption that guarantees the slow Torkoal Eruption or a fast redirected boost
Tapu Lele Psychic Terrain protects the fragile sun setup from Fake Out and priority
antiSynergies: Sun is double-edged: it boosts opposing Fire moves too, and the team carries Fire-weak types (Lilligant is Grass, Buzzwole is part-Bug)
Hard-countered by opposing weather setters (Gigalith Sand, Pelipper Rain, Ninetales hail) that overwrite Drought and neuter Chlorophyll
If Torkoal is removed or Taunted before sun is up, Lilligant loses its Speed and the whole engine stalls

corpus.author: Xtra$hine
