corpus.era: Gen 7 USUM, before Zygarde's Gen 7 OU ban
corpus.strategy: Lead with a Focus Sash suicide setter (Greninja or Excadrill) to lay Spikes + Stealth Rock and Taunt the opposing lead, then overwhelm with a stack of speed-boosting setup sweepers (Dragon Dance Zygarde, Dragon Dance Mega Gyarados, Shift Gear/Calm Mind Magearna, Calm Mind Blacephalon) that each cover one another's checks.

members.role: Dragon Dance sweeper; Thousand Arrows hits Flying/levitators, Extreme Speed is priority. Source lists Aura Break (Power Construct was the more common competitive ability) and the 4th slot as Dragon Tail/Coil/Substitute. Zygarde was later banned from Gen 7 OU.
Suicide Spikes lead; Focus Sash + Taunt guarantees hazards vs opposing leads
Dual-dance (Shift Gear + Calm Mind) setup sweeper and secondary win condition
Dragon Dance sweeper; Mold Breaker Earthquake ignores Levitate. Source gives the 3rd slot as Substitute/Taunt/Waterfall.
Calm Mind + Substitute sweeper; Beast Boost snowballs Speed, Ghostium Z is the breaking nuke
Secondary Focus Sash lead — Stealth Rock setter and Rapid Spin hazard control

synergies: Every sweeper boosts its own Speed (Dragon Dance / Shift Gear / Beast Boost), so whichever survives can clean once its checks are gone
Spikes (Greninja) + Stealth Rock (Excadrill) chip lets the sweepers break sashes and bulky answers
Mold Breaker on Mega Gyarados and Excadrill ignores Levitate and other blocking abilities
Zygarde's Extreme Speed provides priority to pick off faster or weakened threats
antiSynergies: Two Focus Sash leads (Greninja + Excadrill) are partly redundant and both die to hazards, weather or multi-hit chip that breaks the Sash
No defensive backbone at all — the team folds if it loses offensive momentum
Stacked setup sweepers are all cold to fast priority, to phazing/Haze (e.g. Toxapex), and to Unaware walls like Clefable
Relies on Zygarde as a centerpiece, which was subsequently banned from Gen 7 OU, dating the team to a narrow legal window

synergies.name: Redirection shields a fragile setup / restricted (Follow Me & Rage Powder)
synergies.description: In doubles, Follow Me / Rage Powder force every single-target enemy attack onto the user, buying one fully protected turn for a fragile partner to boost, fire a two-turn move, or set up a restricted. It works because the opponent cannot ignore the redirect without spread moves, Safety Goggles, or Sub-breaking chip; the redirector is usually bulky, Regenerator, or Sitrus-healed so it can eat the redirected hits repeatedly.
examples: Follow Me — Se Jun Park's 2014 World Champion team
The 'Big 6' — the format-defining VGC 2016 hyper-offense archetype
James Baek — 'TornOgre History' (Xerneas / Primal Kyogre rain offense)

synergies.name: Draw-in immunity abilities that redirect AND cover a partner's weakness
synergies.description: Lightning Rod / Storm Drain / Volt Absorb pull a whole move type onto an immune body, so the same slot both redirects and papers over a teammate's crippling weakness — letting a 4x-weak restricted or mega sit on the field safely. Telepathy variants dodge the partner's own spread move.
examples: Wolfe Glick — 2016 World Champion (Kyogre/Rayquaza + Lightning Rod Raichu)
Paul Ruiz — 2018 World Champion ("Soaring Higher")
Follow Me — Se Jun Park's 2014 World Champion team

synergies.name: Weather + weather-abuser (speed and power)
synergies.description: A permanent-weather setter (Drizzle/Drought) simultaneously boosts matching STAB and switches on a partner's Swift Swim/Chlorophyll to double its Speed, turning a mid-speed attacker into a fast nuke. Rain also ~doubles Water damage and makes Thunder/Hurricane 100% accurate; sun makes Solar Beam instant. Common to both doubles and singles rain/sun.
examples: What's Protect? — Alex Ogloza's US Nationals Champion team
Manaphy Rain (Ske Rain)
'Dancing in the Sun' — Torkoal / Lilligant sun offense

synergies.name: Weather as defense / weather denial
synergies.description: The same weather that boosts you also erases a category of enemy damage or a teammate weakness: Primordial Sea/rain nullifies Fire, Desolate Land nullifies Water, and setting your own weather overwrites the opponent's — shutting off their Swift Swim/Chlorophyll and their STAB boosts.
examples: Wolfe Glick — 2016 World Champion (Kyogre/Rayquaza + Lightning Rod Raichu)
Shoma Honami's World Champion team ("shadeVIERA")
What's Protect? — Alex Ogloza's US Nationals Champion team

synergies.name: Trick Room + slow, hyper-bulky attackers
synergies.description: Trick Room inverts the speed order for five turns so deliberately-slow, tanky attackers (Aegislash, Snorlax, Heatran, Stakataka, Adamant Primal Groudon) move first and hit hardest while surviving anything. Setters are chosen to be bulky and Fake-Out-immune so they live to flip the field.
examples: Hideyuki Taida's Worlds Runner-Up team ("BIDC")
Naoto Mizobuchi — 2019 World Champion (Primal Groudon / Lunala Trick Room)
Shoma Honami's World Champion team ("shadeVIERA")

synergies.name: Dual / redundant Trick Room setters for reliability
synergies.description: Two independent TR setters make the mode land against Taunt, Fake Out, and focus-fire that would stop a lone setter — you can lose one and still invert speed. The cost is bench slots, but it makes an otherwise all-or-nothing plan dependable.
examples: Aaron Traylor — 2016 Worlds Top 8 (Dual-setter Trick Room Xerneas/Groudon)
Naoto Mizobuchi — 2019 World Champion (Primal Groudon / Lunala Trick Room)

synergies.name: Intimidate stacking
synergies.description: Two or more Intimidate bodies that pivot in and out (Incineroar, Landorus-T, Mega Salamence) repeatedly drop enemy Attack, compounding to neutralize physical offense and protect fragile setup sweepers turn after turn. Primarily a doubles engine, where multiple Intimidate switch-ins are practical.
examples: Paul Ruiz — 2018 World Champion ("Soaring Higher")
Zheyuan 'Ken' Huang — 'The best Xerndon' (Xerneas / Primal Groudon)
20XX Mence (Alberto Lara / CaliSweeper)

synergies.name: Fake Out into free setup / tempo
synergies.description: Fake Out flinches one foe for a guaranteed free turn a partner spends on Belly Drum / Dragon Dance / Geomancy, or that the team uses for positioning in place of Protect. It stacks value when carried on an Intimidate body (Incineroar), giving damage reduction and tempo in one slot.
examples: Paul Ruiz — 2018 World Champion ("Soaring Higher")
Nils Dunlop — 2018 Worlds Top 4 (Mega Metagross balance)
What's Protect? — Alex Ogloza's US Nationals Champion team

synergies.name: Layered non-weather speed control (Tailwind / paralysis / Icy Wind / Electroweb)
synergies.description: Stacking speed control lets a slower core move first without committing to Trick Room: Tailwind doubles team Speed for four turns, Thunder Wave/Nuzzle paralysis halves a threat's Speed, and Icy Wind/Electroweb/Rock Tomb drop enemy Speed while chipping. Often a redundant second layer behind the primary plan.
examples: Follow Me — Se Jun Park's 2014 World Champion team
Paul Ruiz — 2018 World Champion ("Soaring Higher")
Raghav 'Mudhiman' Malaviya — 'RayOgre's Evolution' (Mega Rayquaza / Primal Kyogre)

synergies.name: Priority + anti-priority axis (Gale Wings / Bullet Punch + Quick/Wide Guard)
synergies.description: Fast offense pairs a priority finisher (Gale Wings Brave Bird, Bullet Punch, Extreme Speed) to pick off weakened or faster threats with Quick Guard / Wide Guard to block the opponent's priority and spread moves on the turns it matters — controlling both ends of the priority axis.
examples: When It Rains, It Pours (Jack Hatch / Logilink)
Follow Me — Se Jun Park's 2014 World Champion team
The 'Big 6' — the format-defining VGC 2016 hyper-offense archetype

synergies.name: Screens / Aurora Veil + setup sweepers
synergies.description: Aurora Veil (or Reflect + Light Screen) halves incoming damage for 5–8 turns with Light Clay, converting fragile boosters' setup turns into guaranteed survival so they snowball behind the screen. Snow Warning Ninetales sets Veil in a single slot.
examples: Sam Pandelis — 2017 Worlds Runner-up (Aurora Veil defensive setup)

synergies.name: Terrain + terrain abuser
synergies.description: A Surge ability both boosts matching STAB and unlocks a partner mechanic: Electric Terrain triggers Electric Seed (+Def) and Unburden; Grassy Terrain heals grounded allies and halves Earthquake; Psychic Terrain blocks priority into a setup; Misty Terrain blocks status. A Gen 7+ staple in both formats.
examples: Kokolucha Hyper Offense (Xtra HO / Medi Koko Lucha HO)
Ash-Greninja Balance
Sam Pandelis — 2017 Worlds Runner-up (Aurora Veil defensive setup)

synergies.name: Hazard stacking + wallbreakers
synergies.description: Stacking Stealth Rock + Spikes chips every switch-in so wallbreakers cross KO thresholds they otherwise couldn't and revenge-killers finish through Sashes/Sturdy. A one-sided hazard game pairs stacking with your own Defog so only the opponent eats the chip. Largely a singles pattern.
examples: Ash-Greninja Balance
Manaphy Rain (Ske Rain)
Speed-Boosting Blacephalon Hyper Offense

synergies.name: Trapping to remove a specific answer (Shadow Tag / Pursuit / Magma Storm)
synergies.description: A trapper deletes the one Pokémon that walls your win condition: Shadow Tag holds it in to be KO'd or Perished, Pursuit punishes the forced switch of Psychic/Ghost pivots, Magma Storm chip-locks. This surgically opens a sweep or a Perish/2v2 endgame.
examples: KeldTar Bulky Offense
Emilio Forbes — 2018 Worlds Runner-up (Mega Gengar Perish Trap)
Follow Me — Se Jun Park's 2014 World Champion team

synergies.name: VoltTurn / pivoting momentum
synergies.description: U-turn / Volt Switch (and Parting Shot) deal damage while switching, letting a bulky pivot bring a frail breaker in for free and keep initiative. In singles it wins the momentum war for Choice-locked breakers; in doubles it also repositions the active pair.
examples: Mega Medicham Offense
Kokolucha Hyper Offense (Xtra HO / Medi Koko Lucha HO)
Ash-Greninja Balance

synergies.name: Regenerator pivot core
synergies.description: Two or three Regenerator bodies (Toxapex, Tornadus-T, Amoonguss, Slowbro) recover ~33% every time they pivot out, so they can check the tier repeatedly and reset chip without spending recovery turns — the sustainable backbone of balance in both formats.
examples: Ash-Greninja Balance
Swords Dance Mega Mawile Bulky Offense (Sand)
James Baek — 'TornOgre History' (Xerneas / Primal Kyogre rain offense)

synergies.name: Physical/special wall split + cleric/Wish recovery engine
synergies.description: Full stall pairs a dedicated physical wall with a dedicated special wall so nothing hits an unresisted hole, then keeps them alive with a shared recovery/status-cure engine (Wish-passing, Regenerator, Soft-Boiled, Heal Bell/Natural Cure) and an Unaware body to blank setup.
examples: Mega Aggron Stall
Mega Sableye Stall (The Great Walls)

synergies.name: Magic Bounce + spin (two-way hazard control)
synergies.description: Mega Sableye's Magic Bounce reflects the opponent's hazards, Taunt and status straight back, while a spinner lays and clears on your own side — so stall wins the hazard war it normally fears and stacks its own Toxic Spikes at the same time.
examples: Mega Sableye Stall (The Great Walls)

synergies.name: Anti-setup glue (Unaware / Haze / phazing)
synergies.description: Control answers to enemy boosting: Unaware ignores the foe's stat changes, Haze wipes all boosts, and phazing (Roar/Whirlwind/Dragon Tail) forces the boosted mon out — often into hazard chip. Essential to beat the setup sweepers offense relies on.
examples: Mega Aggron Stall
Ash-Greninja Balance
Speed-Boosting Blacephalon Hyper Offense

synergies.name: Complementary offensive core / unavoidable spread pressure
synergies.description: Two attackers whose STABs together hit the tier for at least neutral, with defensive synergy on the back end — Water + Fairy, Ground/Fire + Fairy, Fighting-spam. In doubles the same idea appears as spread pressure (Heat Wave + Hyper Voice) that damages both foes at once and can't be Protected away by one target.
examples: Zheyuan 'Ken' Huang — 'The best Xerndon' (Xerneas / Primal Groudon)
20XX Mence (Alberto Lara / CaliSweeper)
Mega Medicham Offense

synergies.name: Healing Wish / Wish support for frail breakers
synergies.description: A support mon sacrifices itself (Healing Wish) or passes Wish to fully restore a chipped, recovery-less wallbreaker so it can break a second time — turning a one-shot glass cannon into a repeatable threat.
examples: Mega Medicham Offense

synergies.name: Dancer copies setup (free second sweeper)
synergies.description: Dancer (Oricorio) auto-copies any dance move a teammate uses, so one Quiver Dance boosts two sweepers at once — snowballing two win conditions off a single setup turn.
examples: 'Dancing in the Sun' — Torkoal / Lilligant sun offense

synergies.name: Damage-suppression control making a tank un-KOable (Intimidate + Snarl + Misty)
synergies.description: Stacking Attack drops (Intimidate), Special-Attack drops (Snarl) and status-blocking terrain lowers both offensive categories so a bulky/Assault-Vest attacker effectively cannot be KO'd while it chips the whole enemy team — slowing the game into your favor.
examples: AFK / 'FAKEPG' core (Arcanine-Tapu Fini-Kartana bulky balance)
Zheyuan 'Ken' Huang — 'The best Xerndon' (Xerneas / Primal Groudon)

synergies.name: Anti-engine disruption (Taunt + screen/setup removal)
synergies.description: Taunt shuts off Geomancy, Trick Room, Tailwind, recovery and hazards; Brick Break / screen removal shatters Aurora Veil. Bringing both denies the opponent's engine before it starts and is the standard counter to defensive-setup and setup-lead teams.
examples: Ryota Otsubo — 2017 World Champion (dual-Z Whimsicott tempo offense)
Wolfe Glick — 2016 World Champion (Kyogre/Rayquaza + Lightning Rod Raichu)

antiSynergies.name: Two Mega Stones — only one Mega per battle
antiSynergies.description: A team carrying two mega stones can only Mega-Evolve one Pokémon per game, so half the mega investment sits on the bench every match and the 'other' mega is played as a weaker base form or a pure bluff. It buys matchup flexibility at the cost of a dead slot each game.
examples: Charizard the Benchwarmer — starmetroid's sun team
20XX Mence (Alberto Lara / CaliSweeper)
The 'Big 6' — the format-defining VGC 2016 hyper-offense archetype

antiSynergies.name: Competing weathers overwrite each other
antiSynergies.description: Two weather setters — especially two restricteds like Primordial Sea Kyogre + Desolate Land Groudon, or Mega Rayquaza's Delta Stream — cancel each other's field, so their boosts are mutually exclusive and fielding both together dilutes each. They are really two separate game plans, not a combo.
examples: Jonathan 'Ezrael' Evans — 2016 Worlds Finalist (Double Primal)
Wolfe Glick — 2016 World Champion (Kyogre/Rayquaza + Lightning Rod Raichu)
Raghav 'Mudhiman' Malaviya — 'RayOgre's Evolution' (Mega Rayquaza / Primal Kyogre)

antiSynergies.name: Weather-neutral win condition ignores (or hates) the team's weather
antiSynergies.description: On a dual-win-con team where one condition wants weather and the other is weather-neutral or hurt by it, the two pull opposite directions — the 'off' piece is dead investment that game, and its coverage may even be weakened by the active weather.
examples: When It Rains, It Pours (Jack Hatch / Logilink)
Charizard the Benchwarmer — starmetroid's sun team

antiSynergies.name: Fast mode vs Trick Room mode tension
antiSynergies.description: Carrying both fast pieces and a Trick Room setter forces an all-in choice each game: commit to TR and the fast attackers are stranded moving last; commit to fast and the TR setter and slow attackers are dead weight. If both modes come up at once they actively undercut each other.
examples: Tomohiro Seki — 2018 Worlds Top 8 (Cresselia Trick Room dual-terrain balance)
Nils Dunlop — 2018 Worlds Top 4 (Mega Metagross balance)
Naoto Mizobuchi — 2019 World Champion (Primal Groudon / Lunala Trick Room)

antiSynergies.name: Shared type-weakness stacking
antiSynergies.description: Multiple members sharing a weakness let one attacker or coverage move threaten several at once, and stacking same-type attackers hands their common check a free wall. The team gets punished by a single well-chosen coverage move.
examples: Rain Offense (Pelipper + Mega Swampert + Ash-Greninja)
Kokolucha Hyper Offense (Xtra HO / Medi Koko Lucha HO)
Mega Charizard X Hyper Offense

antiSynergies.name: Attacker-spam walled by one shared check
antiSynergies.description: Two attackers of the same offensive type overwhelm nothing if a single bulky answer resists both — Fighting-spam collapses into a bulky Psychic/Fairy — so the whole offense can be stonewalled unless a trapper removes that check first.
examples: Mega Medicham Offense
KeldTar Bulky Offense

antiSynergies.name: 4x or crippling weakness on a linchpin
antiSynergies.description: A key piece with a 4x weakness (or a crippling single weakness) is a pressure point the opponent aims at, forcing the team to spend a whole slot (Shuca Berry, a dedicated redirector) papering over it — and if that patch is removed, the linchpin folds.
examples: Shoma Honami's World Champion team ("shadeVIERA")
Follow Me — Se Jun Park's 2014 World Champion team
Swords Dance Mega Mawile Bulky Offense (Sand)

antiSynergies.name: Single point of failure (centralized enabler)
antiSynergies.description: When one enabler — weather setter, redirector, screen setter, sole hazard setter — props up the entire plan, removing it collapses the team. The more centralized the engine, the more it inverts into a liability the opponent can target directly.
examples: What's Protect? — Alex Ogloza's US Nationals Champion team
'Dancing in the Sun' — Torkoal / Lilligant sun offense
Sam Pandelis — 2017 Worlds Runner-up (Aurora Veil defensive setup)

antiSynergies.name: No Protect / no scouting in doubles
antiSynergies.description: Forgoing Protect across a doubles team removes the tool used to scout, dodge double-targeting, stall out Trick Room/Tailwind turns, and pivot safely — leaving the team acutely exposed to spread damage and focus-fire even as it wins the raw-tempo race.
examples: What's Protect? — Alex Ogloza's US Nationals Champion team

antiSynergies.name: No hazard removal on a hazard-vulnerable offense
antiSynergies.description: A frail, switch-heavy offense with no Defog/Rapid Spin (or only a fragile one) is ground down by Stealth Rock + Spikes across its many forced switches — especially recovery-less breakers that can't afford the chip. A singles failure mode.
examples: KeldTar Bulky Offense
Kokolucha Hyper Offense (Xtra HO / Medi Koko Lucha HO)
Mega Medicham Offense

antiSynergies.name: Redundant roles / duplicated tools
antiSynergies.description: Two members doing the same job waste a slot that could patch a weakness — two Focus Sash suicide leads, double Defog, overlapping win conditions. Redundancy is only good when it is deliberate insurance (dual TR setters); here it is just wasted coverage.
examples: Speed-Boosting Blacephalon Hyper Offense
Mega Aggron Stall

antiSynergies.name: Choice-lock exploitability
antiSynergies.description: Choice items lock the holder into one move, so a well-timed switch to an immune or resistant pivot earns a free turn and momentum; stacking several Choice users multiplies the read risk and lets bulky pivots dance around the team.
examples: KeldTar Bulky Offense
Emilio Forbes — 2018 Worlds Runner-up (Mega Gengar Perish Trap)
Paul Ruiz — 2018 World Champion ("Soaring Higher")

antiSynergies.name: Conditional win condition the opponent can decline
antiSynergies.description: A plan that needs the opponent to cooperate — land a super-effective hit (Weakness Policy), stay trapped in, or forgo Safety Goggles — can simply be played around by a careful pilot, so the payoff never comes online.
examples: Hideyuki Taida's Worlds Runner-Up team ("BIDC")
Follow Me — Se Jun Park's 2014 World Champion team

antiSynergies.name: Redirection / Sub-trap broken by spread, Snarl, or Safety Goggles
antiSynergies.description: Redirection and Substitute-based trapping are defeated by spread moves that hit through the redirect, by Snarl/multi-hit chip that breaks the Substitute keeping a trap online, or by Safety Goggles that ignore powder — and opponents pack exactly this tech, so the fragile engine can be dismantled at the wrong moment.
examples: Emilio Forbes — 2018 Worlds Runner-up (Mega Gengar Perish Trap)
Follow Me — Se Jun Park's 2014 World Champion team

antiSynergies.name: Passive control/stall outraced by hyper offense
antiSynergies.description: Slow grind plans (stall, Perish Trap, damage-suppression balance) can be overwhelmed before they stabilize if the opponent simply KOs through them — elite wallbreakers, Taunt, Mold Breaker hazard-setters, and all-out offense race past the control engine.
examples: Emilio Forbes — 2018 Worlds Runner-up (Mega Gengar Perish Trap)
Mega Sableye Stall (The Great Walls)
AFK / 'FAKEPG' core (Arcanine-Tapu Fini-Kartana bulky balance)

antiSynergies.name: Boost-reliant / slow-to-online offense loses the race
antiSynergies.description: Win conditions that need a setup turn (Geomancy, Tail Glow, Belly Drum, Quiver Dance) are dead weight until online — fast Taunt, priority, or being out-sped punishes the tempo cost, and even at +6 a naturally slow sweeper still needs Tailwind/Trick Room to actually move first.
examples: Sam Pandelis — 2017 Worlds Runner-up (Aurora Veil defensive setup)
Paul Ruiz — 2018 World Champion ("Soaring Higher")
James Baek — 'TornOgre History' (Xerneas / Primal Kyogre rain offense)

antiSynergies.name: Weather war — the opponent's weather flips your abuser
antiSynergies.description: Only one weather can be active, so an opposing setter overwrites yours and instantly turns off Swift Swim/Chlorophyll, kills your STAB boosts, and may even power the foe. Weather offense lives and dies on winning that setter war.
examples: What's Protect? — Alex Ogloza's US Nationals Champion team
Manaphy Rain (Ske Rain)
'Dancing in the Sun' — Torkoal / Lilligant sun offense

antiSynergies.name: Weather / terrain is double-edged
antiSynergies.description: A field effect you set also helps the opponent — sun boosts their Fire moves (and burns your own Fire-weak mons), terrain empowers their matching STAB — and weather/terrain timers lapse at inconvenient moments, especially when the setter can't hold an extender item.
examples: 'Dancing in the Sun' — Torkoal / Lilligant sun offense
Kokolucha Hyper Offense (Xtra HO / Medi Koko Lucha HO)

antiSynergies.name: Setup-sweeper stack cold to Haze / Unaware / phazing / priority
antiSynergies.description: A team built entirely on boosting sweepers has no fallback against a single Haze/Unaware/phazer or fast priority — one blank of the boosts and the whole win condition evaporates because there is no defensive backbone to buy a reset.
examples: Speed-Boosting Blacephalon Hyper Offense
The 'Big 6' — the format-defining VGC 2016 hyper-offense archetype

antiSynergies.name: Incidental weather chips your own team
antiSynergies.description: Weather that isn't fueling a sweeper still deals residual chip to your own non-immune members, partly undoing Regenerator recovery and Leftovers — so the weather quietly works against the team it is on.
examples: Swords Dance Mega Mawile Bulky Offense (Sand)

antiSynergies.name: Four-moveslot syndrome / slot tension
antiSynergies.description: A single Pokémon wants more than four moves — STAB, coverage, speed control, and utility — so builders slash options and leave a matchup gap whichever they cut. Support mons trying to do offense + speed control + protection feel it most, and Choice/utility combos strain the slots further.
examples: When It Rains, It Pours (Jack Hatch / Logilink)
KeldTar Bulky Offense
Ash-Greninja Balance

singles.name: Balance
singles.description: A defensive pivot core (usually Regenerator users) that soaks hits and generates momentum for one or two elite Choice wallbreakers, tilts the hazard game in its favor, and closes with a setup/Z-move win condition. Trades the all-in speed of hyper offense for resilience and staying power.
singles.format: Smogon singles OU (Gen 6 ORAS, Gen 7 SM/USUM)
singles.structure: 2-3 bulky/Regenerator pivots (e.g. Toxapex, Tornadus-T, Celesteela) + 1 Stealth Rock setter + 1 Defog hazard-remover + 1-2 Choice wallbreakers (Specs/Band) + 1 setup or Z-move win condition; VoltTurn/U-turn momentum feeds the breakers in safely, with a one-sided hazard game (own Spikes/SR up, opponent's Defogged away).
examples: Ash-Greninja Balance (Finchinator)

singles.name: Bulky Offense
singles.description: An offensively slanted team that keeps a small defensive backbone (a wall or two plus Intimidate/pivots) so its wallbreakers and a Choice-Scarf revenge killer can apply relentless pressure. Frequently pairs a Pursuit trapper to delete the specific walls that answer the main breaker, then closes with a setup or priority sweeper.
singles.format: Smogon singles OU (Gen 6 ORAS, Gen 7 SM/USUM)
singles.structure: 1-2 defensive anchors/pivots (bulky Water, Steel, Intimidate Landorus-T) + Stealth Rock setter + Choice-Scarf revenge killer/pivot + 1-2 wallbreakers (Choice item or Mega) + optional Pursuit trapper (Tyranitar) to open the breaker's path + a setup/priority win condition. Sand (Tyranitar Sand Stream) may be added incidentally for bulk and Pursuit chip rather than as a true weather engine.
examples: KeldTar Bulky Offense
Mega Medicham Offense
Swords Dance Mega Mawile Bulky Offense (Sand)

singles.name: Hyper Offense
singles.description: Maximum aggression with little or no defensive backbone: a Focus-Sash suicide lead lays hazards and Taunts the opposing lead, then a stack of speed-boosting setup sweepers (or terrain-enabled sweepers) each cover one another's checks and race to win before the opponent stabilizes.
singles.format: Smogon singles OU (Gen 6 ORAS, Gen 7 SM/USUM)
singles.structure: 1 (sometimes 2) Focus-Sash suicide hazard leads (Spikes + Stealth Rock, Taunt) + 3-4 speed-boosting setup sweepers (Dragon Dance / Shift Gear / Calm Mind / Unburden) + priority and/or VoltTurn momentum. A common variant is built around a terrain setter (Tapu Koko Electric Terrain) that triggers a Seed/Unburden sweeper (Hawlucha) for the win condition.
examples: Speed-Boosting Blacephalon Hyper Offense
Kokolucha Hyper Offense (Electric-Terrain sweep)
Mega Charizard X Hyper Offense

singles.name: Full Stall (Defensive)
singles.description: A near-passive defensive wall of a team that splits the physical/special defensive load across dedicated walls, denies the opponent's win conditions (Unaware vs setup, Magic Bounce vs hazards/status), and grinds foes down with Toxic/Toxic Spikes/Will-O-Wisp + Seismic Toss while sustaining itself with Wish, Recover, Regenerator and clerics.
singles.format: Smogon singles OU (Gen 6 ORAS, Gen 7 SM/USUM)
singles.structure: 1 dedicated physical wall + 1 special wall (Eviolite Chansey) + 1 Unaware setup-check (Clefable) + 1 cleric/Wish passer + hazard control (Defog, sometimes doubled) + Stealth Rock setter + a status/residual-damage win condition. Keystone is a bounce/immunity ability: Magic Bounce (Mega Sableye) or Filter (Mega Aggron).
examples: Mega Sableye Stall
Mega Aggron Stall

singles.name: Rain (Weather) Offense
singles.description: A weather team built around Drizzle: the setter turns on rain to double a Swift Swim sweeper's Speed, power up largely unresisted Water attacks (Choice Specs), and give 100%-accurate Hurricanes, while a rain-independent setup sweeper and a Grass/Steel defensive glue keep the plan alive after rain lapses. The clearest singles 'gimmick/weather' archetype in the corpus.
singles.structure: 1 Drizzle setter (Pelipper, Damp Rock for 8 turns) + 1 Swift Swim sweeper (Mega Swampert) + 1 rain-boosted special nuke (Choice Specs Ash-Greninja) + 1 rain-independent win condition (Manaphy / Magearna) + 1 defensive glue covering Grass/Electric (Ferrothorn) + a Defog/Regenerator pivot (Tornadus-T). Hazard stacking (Spikes) supports the Water spam.
examples: Manaphy Rain (Ske Rain)
Rain Offense (Pelipper + Mega Swampert + Ash-Greninja)

doubles.name: Bulky Offense (Intimidate/Fake Out core)
doubles.description: The default VGC framework: a support core built on Intimidate and Fake Out (Incineroar, Landorus-T, Mega Salamence) blunts the opponent's damage to open a safe turn for a setup sweeper or spread attackers to take over, with Tailwind (or a Trick Room backup) as speed control. Offensively slanted but carries real bulk and a clear win condition.
doubles.format: VGC doubles (2014-2019, Gen 6-7; incl. restricted formats)
doubles.structure: 1-2 Intimidate/Fake Out support (Incineroar, Landorus-T, Mega Salamence) + 2 spread/attacking mons + 1 setup win condition (Belly Drum Snorlax, Dragon Dance Salamence, Geomancy Xerneas) + speed control (Tailwind) + a defensive pivot. In restricted formats this collapses onto a synergistic restricted core (e.g. XernDon = Xerneas + Primal Groudon).
examples: Paul Ruiz's 2018 World Champion team (Soaring Higher)
Zheyuan Huang's 2019 XernDon
Ryota Otsubo's 2017 World Champion team

doubles.name: Balance / Dual-Mode Goodstuffs
doubles.description: A flexible, matchup-adaptive team of individually strong Pokémon that carries two win conditions and two speed-control modes (a fast Tailwind/terrain mode and a slow Trick Room mode) over a bulky Intimidate/redirection backbone. The pilot reads Team Preview and commits to the mode that beats the opponent rather than to a single fixed plan.
doubles.structure: Fake Out/Intimidate support (Incineroar / Mega Kangaskhan) + 2 flexible attackers + a fast-mode speed setter (Tailwind or terrain Tapu) + a slow-mode Trick Room setter (Cresselia) + a terrain/weather setter or redirector; a bulky backbone (Assault Vest, Regenerator) so the team can safely pivot into whichever mode wins.
examples: Shoma Honami's 2015 World Champion team (shadeVIERA)
Tomohiro Seki's 2018 Top 8 team
AFK / FAKEPG core

doubles.name: Hyper Offense / Goodstuffs
doubles.description: An all-out damage race with minimal defensive investment (the famous 'no Protect' builds): a lead pair applies unavoidable spread damage, or a Fake Out + Follow Me support guarantees a setup sweeper's boost turn, backed by a priority 'win button' and Tailwind so the team KOs faster than the opponent can stabilize.
doubles.structure: Fast spread-damage lead pair (or Fake Out + Follow Me support enabling a one-turn setup sweeper such as Geomancy Xerneas) + 1-2 hard-hitting attackers/Mega + a Gale Wings priority attacker (Talonflame) as the win button + Tailwind speed control; Choice/offensive items favored over Protect to maximize output.
examples: The 'Big 6'
Alex Ogloza's What's Protect? (2014 US Nationals champion)
James Baek's 2019 TornOgre rain offense

doubles.name: Weather Offense (Sun / Rain)
doubles.description: Built around a weather setter that both powers its own side and cripples the opponent's: Drizzle/Primal rain doubles Swift Swim Speed and boosts Water nukes; Drought/Primal sun powers Fire and doubles Chlorophyll Speed. A redirector or support core shields the fragile setter, and a weather-independent backup win condition covers weather wars.
doubles.format: VGC doubles (2014-2019, Gen 6-7; incl. restricted Primal weather)
doubles.structure: 1 weather setter (Politoed/Primal Kyogre for rain; Torkoal/Charizard-Y/Primal Groudon for sun) + 1-2 weather abusers (Swift Swim or Chlorophyll sweeper, or a weather-boosted spread nuke like Water Spout/Eruption) + support to protect the setter (Fake Out, redirection, Intimidate) + speed control (Tailwind) + a weather-independent backup attacker.
examples: Alex Ogloza's What's Protect? (rain)
Dancing in the Sun — Torkoal/Lilligant (sun)
Wolfe Glick's 2016 World Champion Primal Kyogre team

doubles.name: Trick Room
doubles.description: Inverts the speed order for a few turns so deliberately slow, high-power attackers move first. Runs one or (for reliability against Taunt/pressure) two Trick Room setters plus slow wallbreakers that abuse it, with redirection/protection to survive the setup turn; the strongest builds also carry a non-TR (Tailwind) fallback mode.
doubles.structure: 1-2 Trick Room setters (Cresselia, Bronzong, Lunala, Stakataka, or Disguise-guaranteed Mimikyu) + 2-3 slow high-Attack/SpA abusers (Aegislash, Volcarona, Primal Groudon, Stakataka) + redirection/protection support (Amoonguss Rage Powder, Wide Guard) + often a fast Tailwind backup mode. Ability-manipulation tech (Skill Swap, Ally Switch) commonly rides on the setters.
examples: Naoto Mizobuchi's 2019 World Champion team (Primal Groudon / Lunala)
Aaron Traylor's 2016 Top 8 (dual-setter TR Xerneas/Groudon)
Hideyuki Taida's 2015 Runner-up (Weakness Policy TR setup)

doubles.name: Redirection Support Offense
doubles.description: A support Pokémon soaks the opponent's attacks off a fragile win condition using Follow Me / Rage Powder or an absorbing ability (Lightning Rod, Storm Drain), buying free turns for a setup sweeper or a glass-cannon nuke, while chip/paralysis support softens targets for the clean-up.
doubles.structure: 1 redirector (Follow Me Pachirisu, Rage Powder Amoonguss, Lightning Rod Raichu/Manectric, Storm Drain Gastrodon) + 1-2 win conditions it shelters (Dragon Dance Mega Gyarados, Geomancy Xerneas, Primal Kyogre) + chip/status support (Nuzzle, Super Fang, Fake Out, paralysis) + speed control + a protective pivot. The absorbing-ability version also patches the sweeper's type weakness (e.g. Lightning Rod covering a Water/Flying's Electric weakness).
examples: Se Jun Park's 2014 World Champion Follow Me team
Wolfe Glick's 2016 World Champion team (Lightning Rod Raichu)

doubles.name: Perish Trap / Trapping Control
doubles.description: A control archetype that removes the opponent's escape and win options instead of out-damaging them: a Shadow Tag trapper locks a key Pokémon in place while double Perish Song, or trapping plus Encore/Taunt/Intimidate disruption, grinds the trapped mon to nothing; Substitute/Protect stall out the Perish count.
doubles.structure: 1 Shadow Tag trapper (Mega Gengar / Gothitelle) + a second Perish Song user (Politoed) or a removal/disruption partner + Fake Out/Intimidate/Encore/Icy Wind disruption support + Substitute/Protect to survive and stall the Perish count + a backup attacker for games where the trap plan is answered (e.g. Snarl/spread damage breaking the Substitute).
examples: Emilio Forbes's 2018 Runner-up Mega Gengar Perish Trap

doubles.name: Screens & Tailwind / Field Speed Control
doubles.description: A gimmick built on temporary field advantage rather than raw damage: Aurora Veil (or Light Screen/Reflect) blunts incoming hits so stat-boosting sweepers set up safely behind it, while Tailwind or a Pledge-'swamp' terrain flips the speed war. Trades immediate power for a handful of protected turns that the win conditions snowball out of.
doubles.structure: 1 field-control setter (Aurora Veil Alolan Ninetales + Light Clay, or Water+Grass Pledge 'swamp', or a dedicated Tailwind user) + 2-3 setup sweepers that boost behind it (Swords Dance, Tail Glow, Calm Mind, Quiver Dance) + Intimidate/speed-control support + a priority/Taunt disruptor to protect the setup turns + a second speed-control layer (Tailwind) as backup.
examples: Sam Pandelis's 2017 Runner-up Aurora Veil team
The Power of Pledge — Serapis's swamp-terrain offense

findings.subject: Fake Out
findings.observation: Fake Out appears on nearly every VGC champion/finalist team in the corpus (Incineroar in 2018/2019 winners, Mega Kangaskhan, Ludicolo, Hitmontop, Raichu, Smeargle). Its value is a guaranteed turn-1 flinch that buys a free setup/positioning turn (Belly Drum Snorlax, Geomancy Xerneas), not damage. In rating.js it is a 40-BP damage move scored by rateMove (~10*40/140 = 2.9) plus the +3 EFFECT_FIRST_TURN_ONLY bump = ~5.9, and it is NOT in DOUBLES_SUPPORT_RATINGS, so rateMoveDoubles gives it no doubles floor.
findings.suggestion: Add MOVE_FAKE_OUT to DOUBLES_SUPPORT_RATINGS with a floor around 7.5-8 (peer to Follow Me/Tailwind). It is arguably the single most ubiquitous doubles support move and is currently under-rated in the exact format it dominates.

findings.subject: Snarl / Fake Tears (spread special-attack drop)
findings.observation: Spread SpA-drop support is a defining doubles win condition: Incineroar/Arcanine Snarl weaken both foes' special damage (Paul Ruiz's 2018 title and the AFK core are built on it), and Fake Tears enables special sweeps. In rateMove a spread stat-drop on the *target* is treated as a downside (hasSpaDrop -> rating *= 0.9), and Snarl gets only the generic spread damage multiplier (1.35x on ~55 BP ~= 5.3). Neither Snarl nor Fake Tears has a DOUBLES_SUPPORT_RATINGS floor.
findings.suggestion: In the doubles path, treat a spread offensive stat-drop as a bonus rather than a penalty, and give MOVE_SNARL / MOVE_FAKE_TEARS / MOVE_STRUGGLE_BUG a doubles floor ~6-7. The category-suppression they provide is a primary strategy, not chip.

findings.subject: Icy Wind / Electroweb / Bulldoze / Rock Tomb (spread speed control)
findings.observation: Persistent, item-free spread speed control is an entire doubles axis parallel to Tailwind/Trick Room: Cresselia and A-Ninetales Icy Wind, Tapu Koko Electroweb (Mudhiman Top-4 NAIC), Latias Icy Wind, Landorus Rock Tomb. These are low-BP moves (~55/35) so the damage rater scores them low, and rateMove even applies hasSpeDrop -> *= 0.95. The spread speed drop that IS the point is penalized, and none has a doubles floor.
findings.suggestion: Give spread Speed-drop moves a doubles floor (~5.5-6, below Tailwind's 7.5 since it is weaker but repeatable) and remove/invert the speed-drop penalty when the move is a spread move in doubles.

findings.subject: Entry hazards in doubles (Stealth Rock, Spikes, Toxic Spikes, Sticky Web)
findings.observation: The rater rates Stealth Rock 8, Sticky Web 8, Toxic Spikes 6.5, Spikes 6 in statusList, and rateMoveDoubles passes these through unchanged (no spread, no doubles floor entry) so they keep their high singles score. In doubles/VGC (bring 6 / pick 4, far fewer switches) hazards are near-worthless and almost never seen in the corpus of 4-mon doubles teams; they are a singles-only value. This is an OVER-valuation for the doubles format.
findings.suggestion: In rateMoveDoubles, down-weight hazard-setting moves (e.g. multiply hazard ratings by ~0.35-0.5, or cap at ~3). Hazards are the clearest 'great in singles, dead in doubles' case.

findings.subject: Priority-granting abilities (Prankster, Gale Wings, Triage)
findings.observation: These abilities are not in DOUBLES_ABILITY_RATINGS and get only their base data rating, yet they define multiple corpus teams. Prankster gives priority to Tailwind/Taunt/Thunder Wave (Thundurus, Whimsicott's terrain Z-Nature Power, Tornadus Tailwind) = decisive tempo. Gale Wings makes Brave Bird a +1 priority 'win button' (Talonflame across 2014-2016 teams). A naive rater sees no stat/damage effect and rates them near zero.
findings.suggestion: Add Prankster / Gale Wings / Triage as doubles ability floors (~6-7) and, ideally, a per-mon combo bonus in computeComboBonus when the mon carries the moves these enable (Prankster + Tailwind/Taunt/T-Wave; Gale Wings + Brave Bird; Triage + drain move).

findings.subject: Taunt
findings.observation: Taunt is rated a flat 5 in statusList regardless of user speed or Prankster. In the corpus fast/Prankster Taunt is a premier disruption tool: Krookodile's anti-setup Taunt was singled out as key to Ryota Otsubo's 2017 Worlds win; Mega Gengar Taunt shut off Geomancy/Trick Room/Tailwind for Wolfe Glick; Thundurus/Tornadus/Mandibuzz/Whimsicott all weaponize it. 5 undersells a move that neutralizes whole strategies (Trick Room teams, redirection, screens, recovery stall).
findings.suggestion: Raise the base Taunt rating to ~6.5 and add a bonus when the user is fast or has Prankster (priority Taunt). Consider a doubles floor since Taunt gates the format's setup/speed-control archetypes.

findings.subject: Aurora Veil
findings.observation: MOVE_AURORA_VEIL sits in the top '0' group of statusList (rated 0), while Light Screen and Reflect are 7.5 each. Aurora Veil is both screens in one move and was the engine of Sam Pandelis's 2017 Worlds runner-up 'veil offense' and a defining defensive-setup archetype. Its only condition is snow/hail, and the rater already tracks hasAbility('SNOW_WARNING') context.
findings.suggestion: Rate Aurora Veil at least as high as a single screen (~7.5), and higher (~9) when the mon or a plausible teammate has Snow Warning. Rating it 0 is almost certainly a straightforward bug/oversight.

findings.subject: Perish Song + trapping abilities (Shadow Tag, Arena Trap, Magnet Pull)
findings.observation: Perish Song is rated 2 in statusList and the trapping abilities are absent from DOUBLES_ABILITY_RATINGS. Together they form Perish Trap, Emilio Forbes's 2018 Worlds runner-up plan (Mega Gengar Shadow Tag + double Perish Song), and Shadow Tag also enabled Se Jun Park's Gothitelle and Wolfe Glick's Gengar to delete answers. A naive rater sees no damage/stat effect for either piece.
findings.suggestion: Raise Perish Song to ~5-6 and give it a large combo bonus when the mon (or team) has a trapping ability or Mean Look/Block; add Shadow Tag/Arena Trap/Magnet Pull as doubles ability floors (~6-7) reflecting the trap-and-remove utility.

findings.subject: Terrain-setting abilities (Electric/Grassy/Misty/Psychic Surge) and terrain moves
findings.observation: The Tapus' Surge abilities are not in DOUBLES_ABILITY_RATINGS and terrain moves are rated a flat 2 in statusList, yet terrain defines the entire Gen-7 corpus (Tapu Koko/Fini/Lele/Bulu are among the most-used mons; Kokolucha HO, Ash-Greninja balance, Tapu Lele priority-block, terrain-Z Whimsicott). Terrain grants team-wide STAB boosts, status/priority immunity, and enables items (Seeds) and Unburden sweeps.
findings.suggestion: Add Electric/Grassy/Misty/Psychic Surge as ability floors (~6-7, relevant in singles too), lift terrain moves above 2, and add combo bonuses for terrain + Seed items / Unburden (Hawlucha) and Psychic Terrain + priority-vulnerable setup.

findings.subject: Speed over-reward vs slow bulky attackers under Trick Room
findings.observation: Trick Room already gets a doubles floor of 7, but the deeper gap is that Trick Room INVERTS the value of Speed: under it, low Speed with high bulk/power is optimal. The corpus is full of deliberately slow win conditions (Snorlax, Stakataka, Torkoal, Cresselia, Bronzong, Primal Groudon-Adamant, Xerneas-Bold) built for TR. A rater that always rewards high Speed will systematically under-rate slow bulky attackers and over-rate raw Speed for these builds.
findings.suggestion: When a team/build carries Trick Room (or when scoring a very slow, bulky, high-Attack mon), stop penalizing low Speed and add a 'TR-abuser' bonus. Treat Speed as double-edged rather than monotonic good.

findings.subject: Weather-abuser roles dependent on a teammate setter (Swift Swim, Chlorophyll, Sand Rush, Slush Rush)
findings.observation: computeComboBonus boosts damage when a mon sets its OWN weather (DRIZZLE/DROUGHT handling), but the corpus's rain/sun cores split the setter and the abuser: Politoed(Drizzle) + Ludicolo/Swampert(Swift Swim), Torkoal(Drought) + Lilligant(Chlorophyll). A per-mon rater sees Swift Swim/Chlorophyll do nothing on their own and under-rates these sweepers, and cannot credit the setter for enabling a partner.
findings.suggestion: Add team-level weather-pairing awareness: if the team has a weather setter, treat matching Swift Swim/Chlorophyll/Sand Rush/Slush Rush mons as having doubled Speed (a large bonus). Absent that, keep them low. This is a team-composition signal the per-mon rater currently misses.

findings.subject: Regenerator
findings.observation: Regenerator (33% heal on switch) is the backbone of the corpus's balance/pivot cores (Toxapex, Tornadus-T, Amoonguss, Slowbro, and the AFK core), making pivots effectively un-wearable and rewarding U-turn/Volt Switch loops. It has no combat-stat effect so a damage-focused rater assigns it little value.
findings.suggestion: Give Regenerator a meaningful ability floor (~6-7) and/or a combo bonus when paired with pivoting moves (U-turn/Volt Switch/Flip Turn/Teleport) or a defensive role, since its value is sustained switching, not a stat.

findings.subject: Unaware
findings.observation: Unaware (ignore the foe's stat boosts) is the stall answer to setup sweepers and a recurring win condition/wall in the corpus (Clefable across ORAS/USUM stall teams). It has no offensive footprint, so a naive rater rates it near zero, missing that it single-handedly counters the boosting archetypes (Calm Mind, Swords Dance, Quiver Dance) that the same corpus shows winning games.
findings.suggestion: Give Unaware a solid rating on bulky mons with recovery (~6-7). It is a defensive counter-strategy, valuable in inverse proportion to how much the rater rewards setup moves elsewhere.

findings.subject: Contrary + self-lowering STAB (Leaf Storm, Overheat, Draco Meteor, Superpower)
findings.observation: rating.js line ~773 has an explicit unresolved '// Drops for contrary' note, and selfLoweringMoves are treated only via white-herb handling. With Contrary, Leaf Storm/Overheat/Draco Meteor/Superpower become +1/+2 boosting nukes (Serperior's Leaf Storm is a snowballing sweep in the ORAS corpus). The rater currently treats these moves' self-drops as a cost even for Contrary users.
findings.suggestion: When the ability is Contrary, invert self-lowering moves from a penalty into a boost (treat Leaf Storm/Overheat/etc. as strong repeatable setup-plus-damage). Resolve the flagged TODO in computeComboBonus.

findings.subject: Disguise / guaranteed-free-turn abilities
findings.observation: Mimikyu's Disguise guarantees a free turn to land Trick Room, Taunt, Destiny Bond, or setup (Nils Dunlop and Mudhiman both used it as a Trick Room / disruption 'panic button'). Functionally it is a built-in Substitute for one hit, but a damage/stat-focused rater sees only a typing gimmick.
findings.suggestion: Give Disguise (and similar free-turn enablers) a bonus, especially when the mon carries a high-value one-shot utility move (Trick Room, Taunt, Destiny Bond, a setup move) whose landing it guarantees.

findings.subject: Spread self-hitting moves negated by teammate immunities (Earthquake, Surf, Heat Wave)
findings.observation: rateMoveDoubles gives ally-hitting spread moves (FOES_AND_ALLY / ALL_BATTLERS, e.g. Earthquake/Surf) a reduced 1.2x vs 1.35x for foes-only spread, to price in friendly fire. But the corpus routinely negates that friendly fire with Flying/Levitate/Ground-immune/Storm Drain/Telepathy partners (Gardevoir Telepathy immune to partner EQ, Lando/Salamence Flying, Rotom/Bronzong Levitate, Gastrodon Storm Drain), making spread EQ a premier move. The rater has no way to grant the full multiplier when the team removes the drawback.
findings.suggestion: When the team/partner is immune to the spread type (Flying/Levitate/Ground-immunity/Telepathy/matching absorb ability), treat ally-hitting spread moves as foes-only (full 1.35x). At minimum, flag Earthquake/Surf as high-value on teams built around Ground/Flying immunities.

findings.subject: Bulky-offense item valuation (Assault Vest, Weakness Policy, Focus Sash on leads, Choice lock)
findings.observation: Corpus item choices reward the opposite of naive heuristics: Assault Vest makes all-attacking bulky mons un-KOable (Kartana, Tapu Koko, Incineroar, Snorlax-class, Raichu, Buzzwole) despite 'losing status moves'; Weakness Policy turns a bulky mon that WANTS to be hit into a +2/+2 sweep (Taida's Aegislash); Focus Sash on frail suicide leads guarantees a hazards/redirection/Dark Void turn (Smeargle, Greninja, Terrakion). A naive item rater tends to penalize 'no status moves' (AV), 'needs to be hit' (WP), and Sash on non-frail mons.
findings.suggestion: In rateItemForAPokemon, reward Assault Vest on movesets that are all-damage + bulky, reward Weakness Policy on bulky setup mons with a super-effective-bait typing, and reward Focus Sash specifically on fast fragile support/lead roles rather than treating these as generic/negative.
